section.title: Gideon Defeats Midian
section.sectionRationale: The call of Gideon now becomes deliverance, but on God's terms. The LORD deliberately cuts Gideon's army from thirty-two thousand to three hundred so that Israel cannot boast that its own hand has saved it. He steadies His fearful leader with an enemy's dream, then wins the battle with trumpets, jars, and torches rather than swords. It is one movement: the victory God engineers to be plainly His own.
section.thesis: God shrinks the army to nothing so that the glory of victory is unmistakably His, strengthening a still-fearful deliverer by an overheard dream and routing a vast host by His own hand through three hundred men with horns and torches, teaching that salvation is the LORD's and boasting belongs to no one but Him.
section.frameNote: Four beats: the army cut to three hundred, verses 1 to 8; the reassuring dream in the enemy camp, verses 9 to 14; the rout by trumpets, jars, and torches, verses 15 to 22; and the pursuit and capture of Midian's princes, verses 23 to 25.

units.label: The army cut to three hundred
units.note: lest Israel boast, my own hand has saved me

verses.text: Early in the morning Jerubbaal (that is, Gideon) and all the men with him camped beside the spring of Harod. And the camp of Midian was north of them in the valley near the hill of Moreh.
Then the LORD said to Gideon, “You have too many men for Me to deliver Midian into their hands, lest Israel glorify themselves over Me, saying, ‘My own hand has saved me.’
Now, therefore, proclaim in the hearing of the men: ‘Whoever is fearful and trembling may turn back and leave Mount Gilead.’” So twenty-two thousand of them turned back, but ten thousand remained.

Then the LORD said to Gideon, “There are still too many men. Take them down to the water, and I will sift them for you there. If I say to you, ‘This one shall go with you,’ he shall go. But if I say, ‘This one shall not go with you,’ he shall not go.”
So Gideon brought the men down to the water, and the LORD said to him, “Separate those who lap the water with their tongues like a dog from those who kneel to drink.”
And the number of those who lapped the water with their hands to their mouths was three hundred men; all the others knelt to drink.
Then the LORD said to Gideon, “With the three hundred men who lapped the water I will {{yasha|save}} you and deliver the Midianites into your hand. But all the others are to go home.”

So Gideon sent the rest of the Israelites to their tents but kept the three hundred men, who took charge of the provisions and rams’ horns of the others. And the camp of Midian lay below him in the valley.

units.label: The reassuring dream in the enemy camp
units.note: the sword of Gideon; God has delivered Midian

verses.text: That night the LORD said to Gideon, “Get up and go down against the camp, for I have delivered it into your hand.
But if you are afraid to do so, then go down to the camp with your servant Purah
and listen to what they are saying. Then your hands will be strengthened to attack the camp.” So he went with Purah his servant to the outposts where armed men were guarding the camp.
Now the Midianites, Amalekites, and all the other people of the east had settled in the valley like a swarm of locusts, and their camels were as countless as the sand on the seashore.

And as Gideon arrived, a man was telling his friend about a {{chalom|dream}}. “Behold, I had a dream,” he said, “and I saw a loaf of barley bread come tumbling into the Midianite camp. It struck the tent so hard that the tent overturned and collapsed.”
His friend replied: “This is nothing less than the sword of Gideon son of Joash, the Israelite. God has delivered Midian and the whole camp into his hand.”

units.label: The rout by trumpets, jars, and torches
units.note: a sword for the LORD and for Gideon

verses.text: When Gideon heard the dream and its interpretation, he bowed in worship. He returned to the camp of Israel and said, “Get up, for the LORD has delivered the camp of Midian into your hand.”
And he divided the three hundred men into three companies and gave each man a {{shofar|ram’s horn}} in one hand and a large jar in the other, containing a torch.
“Watch me and do as I do,” Gideon said. “When I come to the outskirts of the camp, do exactly as I do.
When I and all who are with me blow our horns, then you are also to blow your horns from all around the camp and shout, ‘For the LORD and for Gideon!’”

Gideon and the hundred men with him reached the outskirts of the camp at the beginning of the middle watch, just after the changing of the guard. They blew their horns and broke the jars that were in their hands.

The three companies blew their horns and shattered their jars. Holding the torches in their left hands and the horns in their right hands, they shouted, “A {{cherev|sword}} for the LORD and for Gideon!”
Each Israelite took his position around the camp, and the entire Midianite army fled, crying out as they ran.

And when the three hundred rams’ horns sounded, the LORD set all the men in the camp against one another with their swords. The army fled to Beth-shittah toward Zererah as far as the border of Abel-meholah near Tabbath.

units.label: The pursuit and capture of Midian's princes
units.note: Oreb and Zeeb brought to Gideon

verses.text: Then the men of Israel were called out from Naphtali, Asher, and all Manasseh, and they pursued the Midianites.

Gideon sent messengers throughout the hill country of Ephraim to say, “Come down against the Midianites and seize the waters of the Jordan ahead of them as far as Beth-barah.” So all the men of Ephraim were called out, and they captured the waters of the Jordan as far as Beth-barah.

They also captured Oreb and Zeeb, the two princes of Midian; and they killed Oreb at the rock of Oreb and Zeeb at the winepress of Zeeb. So they pursued the Midianites and brought the heads of Oreb and Zeeb to Gideon on the other side of the Jordan.

yasha.gloss: To save, deliver, rescue. God tells Gideon, with the three hundred men I will save you, deliberately pairing the tiny force with His own promise to save. The saving is God's, done through a remnant so small that no one could credit it to human strength. The root stands behind the names Joshua and Jesus, the LORD who saves.

chalom.gloss: A dream. God steadies His fearful leader by letting him overhear a Midianite's dream of a barley loaf toppling a tent, and its instant interpretation as the sword of Gideon. Even the fears and words of the enemy are in God's hand, marshaled to strengthen the one He has called.

shofar.gloss: A ram's horn or trumpet. Instead of swords the three hundred carry horns; the blast of three hundred shofars in the night signals that this is God's battle, fought with sound and light rather than force of arms, so the victory is plainly the LORD's.

cherev.gloss: A sword. The battle cry is A sword for the LORD and for Gideon, yet the only swords that do the killing are Midian's own, turned against each other by the LORD. The sword of the LORD wins the day without Israel striking a blow, underscoring that salvation is God's work.

basic.context: Gideon and his men camp facing a vast Midianite host. Twice the LORD tells Gideon he has too many soldiers, lest Israel boast that its own hand saved it. First the fearful are sent home, leaving ten thousand; then, at the water, God sifts them down to just three hundred. That night, to steady His still-anxious leader, God sends Gideon to eavesdrop on the enemy, where he hears a man describe a dream of a barley loaf toppling a tent and a friend interpreting it as the sword of Gideon. Gideon worships, then arms his three hundred not with weapons but with horns, jars, and torches. At midnight they surround the camp, blow the horns, smash the jars, raise the torches, and shout; the LORD throws Midian into panic so they kill one another and flee, and Israel pursues and captures the enemy's princes.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative with a clear theological center, stated by God Himself in verse 2: the army is reduced so that Israel cannot boast, my own hand has saved me. The story is not a manual of military tactics or a lesson that lapping water is more spiritual than kneeling; the sifting is simply God's way of choosing a remnant so small that only He can get the credit. What abides is the truth that God saves by His own power, often through weakness, so the glory is His.
basic.originalLanguages: God promises, with the three hundred I will yasha, save, you, tying salvation to a force too small to explain the outcome. He strengthens Gideon by an enemy's chalom, dream, showing even the foe's fears serve His purpose. The three hundred carry the shofar, the ram's horn, not weapons, and their cry is a cherev, a sword, for the LORD and for Gideon, though the LORD makes Midian's own swords do the work.
basic.typology: The victory won by God's power through a tiny, ill-armed band foreshadows the gospel pattern, where God saves through apparent weakness, supremely at the cross. The cry, a sword for the LORD, and the enemy routed without Israel's blade point to the LORD who fights for His people, fulfilled in Christ, who triumphs over the powers not by force but by the weakness of His death.
basic.christ: The whole battle is designed so that salvation is seen to be God's and no one may boast, the very logic of the cross, where God saves in weakness so that all boasting is excluded. Gideon's three hundred with horns and torches prefigure the way God overthrows His enemies through what looks like foolishness and frailty. And the sword for the LORD that wins without Israel striking a blow points to Christ, who defeats sin and death by His own hand, so that our salvation is entirely His gift.
basic.god: God is the true Deliverer here from first to last. He insists on cutting the army so the glory cannot be stolen from Him. He tenderly stoops to His fearful servant, steadying him with an overheard dream rather than rebuking him. And He wins the battle Himself, turning the enemy's swords against each other. He is a jealous God who will not share His glory, and a gentle God who strengthens the weak, and He shows that salvation belongs to Him alone.
basic.crossReferences: First Corinthians 1 says God saves in a way that leaves no room for human boasting. Second Corinthians 4 speaks of treasure in jars of clay, so the power is God's, echoing the smashed jars. Psalm 20 declares that some trust in chariots but we in the name of the LORD. And Zechariah 4 says not by might nor by power, but by My Spirit.
basic.oneStory: In the age of the judges God again saves His people, but He does it in a way that forever marks the victory as His own, shrinking the army to three hundred lest Israel boast. Gideon's strange triumph is one more chapter in the story of a God who saves the weak by His own hand, and it points ahead to the cross, where God overthrows His enemies through weakness so that salvation is his gift and boasting belongs to Him alone.

context.literary: Chapter 7 is the heart of the Gideon narrative, moving from call to conquest. It is built around God's twofold reduction of the army and the memorable night battle with horns, jars, and torches. The reassuring dream sits at the center, a quiet scene of divine tenderness between the tense sifting and the explosive attack. The repeated theme, too many men, and the battle cry, a sword for the LORD, hold the chapter together and interpret its action as God's victory.
context.historical: Midian and its allies were camped in the Jezreel valley, a vast raiding coalition on camels. The spring of Harod and the hill of Moreh locate the armies facing each other across the valley. Gideon's three hundred attacked at the beginning of the middle watch, around midnight, just after the guard changed, when a sudden eruption of three hundred trumpets and blazing torches would suggest a huge army and throw a sleeping camp into chaos. Barley bread was the food of the poor, so the dream of a barley loaf toppling a tent fittingly pictured lowly Israel overthrowing mighty Midian.
context.canonical: The reduction of Gideon's army becomes a lasting emblem of God's way of saving, that no flesh should boast in His presence. The battle joins Israel's holy wars, where the LORD Himself fights and the victory is His. Psalm 83 recalls the fall of Oreb and Zeeb as a byword for God's defeat of His foes, and Isaiah 9 remembers the day of Midian as a picture of the light and liberation the Messiah brings. The pattern culminates in the New Testament gospel of salvation by grace through weakness.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that the LORD deliberately made Israel's force too small to explain the victory, so that the deliverance from Midian would be seen as His own saving work and Israel could never boast that its own hand had saved it.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a unique, God-directed battle; it does not prescribe its tactics, nor teach that lapping water rather than kneeling marks the more faithful. The sifting is simply God's chosen means of reducing the army. What is prescriptive is the enduring truth: salvation is the LORD's, He often works through weakness, and the glory must go to Him alone.

debates.question: Why did God choose the three hundred who lapped the water rather than those who knelt?

views.label: A test of alertness or readiness
views.case: Some hold that lapping from the hand kept the men watchful and battle-ready while the kneelers were more exposed, so the method selected the more suitable soldiers for a night raid.

views.label: A simple, arbitrary means of reducing the number
views.case: The text gives no reason and does not praise the lappers; the point, stated in verse 2, is only to shrink the army so drastically that God alone gets the credit, and the manner of sifting is incidental.

debates.bearing: Either way the emphasis falls not on the men's quality but on God's purpose to reduce them; the passage is about God's glory, not a lesson in choosing the fittest soldiers or the most spiritual posture for drinking.

debates.question: Why did God let Gideon overhear the enemy's dream?

views.label: A concession to Gideon's lingering fear
views.case: God explicitly says, if you are afraid, verse 10; the errand to the camp is a gracious accommodation to a frightened leader, strengthening his hands before the battle.

views.label: A confirmation that the outcome is already decided by God
views.case: The dream and its instant interpretation, God has delivered Midian into his hand, show that even the enemy senses the battle is lost; the scene declares God's sovereignty over the result more than it comments on Gideon's courage.

debates.bearing: Both readings hold together and magnify grace: God stoops to steady a fearful servant and, in doing so, reveals that the victory is already His gift, received by faith before it is seen.

study.originalLanguages: The vocabulary keeps the credit with God. When only three hundred remain, the LORD says, with the three hundred men who lapped the water I will yasha, save, you; salvation is deliberately tied to a force far too small to account for the result, so no one can say my own hand has saved me, the very boast God names in verse 2. To steady a still-fearful Gideon, God uses a chalom, a dream, in the enemy camp, a barley loaf toppling a tent, interpreted at once as the sword of Gideon; even the fears and words of the foe are marshaled by God to strengthen the one He has called. The three hundred go into battle carrying the shofar, the ram's horn, and jars and torches, not weapons, so that the assault is sound and light rather than force of arms. Their cry is a cherev, a sword, for the LORD and for Gideon, yet the only swords that kill are Midian's own, which the LORD turns against each other in the dark. Save, dream, horn, and sword, the words themselves announce that this deliverance is the LORD's from beginning to end.

typology.type: Victory won through a tiny, ill-armed band so no one can boast
typology.fulfillment: The salvation of the cross, where God saves in weakness so that all human boasting is excluded, 1 Corinthians 1:27-31.

typology.type: The smashed jars that release the light
typology.fulfillment: The treasure of the gospel in jars of clay, so the surpassing power belongs to God and not to us, 2 Corinthians 4:6-7.

typology.type: The sword for the LORD that routs the enemy without Israel's blade
typology.fulfillment: Christ, who defeats the powers of darkness by His own hand through the weakness of His death, Colossians 2:15.

study.christ: God shapes the entire battle so that the victory can only be credited to Him, cutting the army until Israel cannot say, my own hand has saved me. This is precisely the logic of the gospel: God chose what is weak and foolish in the world so that no one may boast before Him, and He saves through the apparent weakness of the cross, 1 Corinthians 1:27-31. The three hundred with horns and torches, and the light that blazes out when the clay jars are smashed, picture the way God overthrows His enemies through what looks like frailty; the same treasure now shines through jars of clay so the power is plainly God's, 2 Corinthians 4:7. And the cry, a sword for the LORD, answered by an enemy destroyed without Israel lifting a blade, points to Christ, who disarmed the powers and triumphed over them by His own hand at the cross, Colossians 2:15, so that our salvation is entirely His gift and our boasting is only in Him.
study.god: This chapter is a sustained portrait of God as the true and jealous Deliverer. He refuses to let the army stay large, twice declaring you have too many men, because He will not share His glory with human strength or allow Israel the lie that its own hand has saved it. Yet the same God is gentle with weakness, stooping to His frightened leader with an errand to overhear a dream rather than a rebuke, strengthening his hands before the fight. And He wins the battle Himself, throwing the vast host into panic so that Midian's own swords do the work while Israel mainly watches and pursues. He is the God who saves by His own power, who is tender toward the fearful, and who arranges His deliverances so that all the glory returns to Him alone.

crossReferences.note: God chose the weak and foolish things so that no one may boast before Him, the exact purpose God states in reducing Gideon's army.
We have this treasure in jars of clay, so the surpassing power belongs to God, echoing the light released from the smashed jars.
Some trust in chariots and some in horses, but we trust in the name of the LORD, the confidence Gideon's three hundred embody.
God breaks the yoke of Israel's burden as on the day of Midian, remembering this victory as a picture of the salvation the Messiah brings.

study.oneStory: In the dark age of the judges God saves His people again, but He does it in a way that forever stamps the victory as His own, shrinking the army to three hundred lest Israel boast, and routing a vast host by horns, jars, and torches while the LORD turns the enemy's swords on themselves. Gideon's strange triumph belongs to the long story of a God who saves the weak by His own hand, and it points ahead to the cross, where God overthrows sin and death through weakness, so that salvation is His gift and all boasting is excluded, and Isaiah can call the coming rescue a new day of Midian.

quiz.q: Why did the LORD reduce Gideon's army from thirty-two thousand to three hundred?

options.text: So Israel could not boast that its own hand had saved it, and the glory would be God's alone.
options.note: Verse 2 states the reason plainly; the reduction guards God's glory.

options.text: So the victory would clearly be God's doing rather than Israel's strength.
options.note: True, but it omits the specific danger God names, Israel boasting my own hand has saved me.

options.text: Because most of the soldiers were untrained and unfit to fight.
options.note: The issue was not their fitness but the danger of Israel taking the credit.

options.text: Because there was not enough food to supply a large army.
options.note: The text gives God's stated reason, not a shortage of provisions.

quiz.q: How did God strengthen Gideon before the battle?

options.text: He let Gideon overhear a Midianite's dream and its interpretation as the sword of Gideon.
options.note: Verses 9-14 show God steadying His fearful leader through the enemy's own words.

options.text: He sent Gideon into the enemy camp to gather courage.
options.note: Gideon did go down to the camp, but the strengthening came through the overheard dream.

options.text: He gave Gideon a large, well-equipped army to lead.
options.note: The opposite: God had just cut the army to three hundred.

options.text: He promised Gideon he would face no danger at all.
options.note: God acknowledged Gideon's fear rather than removing all danger, verse 10.

quiz.q: With what did Gideon's three hundred men go into battle?

options.text: Ram's horns, jars, and torches, not swords, so the victory would plainly be the LORD's.
options.note: Verse 16 shows the strange weapons that made the deliverance visibly God's work.

options.text: Trumpets and torches to surprise the enemy at night.
options.note: True, but it omits the jars and the point that they carried no ordinary weapons.

options.text: Swords and shields, trusting their training.
options.note: They carried horns, jars, and torches, not swords; the killing was Midian's own.

options.text: Chariots and horses like the surrounding nations.
options.note: Israel had no such force here; the battle was won by God, not by arms.

quiz.q: Who actually defeated the Midianite army?

options.text: The LORD, who set the panicked Midianites against one another with their own swords.
options.note: Verse 22 credits the LORD, who turned the enemy's swords on themselves.

options.text: Gideon's three hundred, whose surprise attack routed the camp.
options.note: The three hundred triggered the panic, but the text says the LORD did the defeating.

options.text: The tribes of Naphtali, Asher, and Manasseh in the pursuit.
options.note: They joined the pursuit afterward, but the rout itself was the LORD's work.

options.text: Gideon alone in single combat.
options.note: The battle was won by God throwing the whole camp into confusion, not by single combat.

quiz.q: How does Gideon's victory point to the gospel?

options.text: God saves through weakness so no one can boast, foreshadowing the cross where salvation is entirely His gift.
options.note: The logic of verse 2 matches 1 Corinthians 1:27-31 and the weakness of the cross.

options.text: It teaches that clever tactics can overcome a stronger enemy.
options.note: The chapter credits God's power, not tactics; the weapons were horns and torches.

options.text: It shows that God helps those who first prove their own strength.
options.note: God removed Israel's strength on purpose so the saving would be His.

options.text: It proves that large numbers are the key to victory.
options.note: God did the opposite, shrinking the army so the glory would be His.

quiz.q: What does the battle cry, a sword for the LORD and for Gideon, reveal?

options.text: That the victory belongs to the LORD, who fights for His people, though His servant is named alongside Him.
options.note: The LORD wins the battle; the cry puts His name first, and Midian's own swords do the work.

options.text: That Gideon and the LORD shared the credit equally.
options.note: Gideon is named, but the chapter insists the saving is God's, lest Israel boast.

options.text: That the men trusted mainly in Gideon's leadership.
options.note: The cry names the LORD first, and the account credits Him, not Gideon's leadership.

options.text: That Israel won by the strength of its own swords.
options.note: Israel struck no blow in the rout; the LORD turned Midian's swords on itself.

questions: God told Gideon he had too many men, lest Israel boast. Where in your life might God be stripping away your resources so that His help, not your strength, gets the glory?
Before the battle, God gently strengthened His fearful servant with an overheard dream rather than a rebuke. How does it comfort you that God stoops to steady the afraid rather than shaming them?
The three hundred went to war with horns and torches, not swords. Where is God asking you to obey in a way that will only work if He shows up?
The victory came when the men blew the horns and smashed the jars, and the LORD did the rest. What does it look like for you to do the small, obedient thing and leave the outcome to God?
Salvation was arranged so no one could say, my own hand has saved me. How does the truth that your salvation in Christ is entirely God's gift free you from boasting and from despair?

translationNotes.note: Jerubbaal is the name Gideon received in the previous chapter; the BSB footnote notes it probably means let Baal contend, recalling the torn-down altar of Judges 6:32.
The BSB footnote gives the more literal sense, rams' horns and empty jars, large jars with torches inside, in the hand of all; the strange arms are central to the scene.
Toward Zererah renders a place name the BSB footnote notes some Hebrew manuscripts read as Zeredah; it traces the route of the fleeing Midianite army.
Oreb means raven and Zeeb means wolf; the rock of Oreb and the winepress of Zeeb were later named for where the two princes fell, remembered in Psalm 83:11 and Isaiah 10:26.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Judges 7:1-25. Chapter 7 is prose narrative. No em or en dashes present in the source or introduced; none anywhere in the file.
